topic: A neighbor asks to use part of the yard for a garden
raw_output:
[story 1]

The invitation hung in the air, thick and saccharine, smelling faintly of overripe tomatoes. Mrs. Henderson, whose garden was a riot of aggressive color and unapologetic growth, had approached my fence with a smile that didn't quite reach her eyes. "Oh, Mark," she'd chirped, gesturing toward a patch of overgrown weeds near the property line, "I was hoping you might let me borrow a little space for some herbs. It would be wonderful." I felt a knot tighten in my chest, a cold, hard pressure that made my jaw ache. I stared at the patch—a neglected, untamed corner of my otherwise orderly lawn—and felt a sudden, intense urge to retreat indoors, to lock the door and forget the sound of her voice. Every time I glanced out the window, I saw the potential invasion, the creeping encroachment of her vibrant, messy world into my quiet space.

[story 2]

I stood by the mailbox, my hands gripping the metal so tightly my knuckles were white. When Mr. Peterson knocked, the sound seemed amplified in the stillness of the afternoon. "Excuse me, Sarah," he began, his voice unusually bright, "I was wondering if I could utilize a small section of your yard for a vegetable patch. It’s just a few feet, nothing major." My breath hitched. I took a slow, deliberate step back, my gaze flicking from his expectant face to the patch of grass he was implicitly requesting. A tremor ran through my legs, making me unsteady. I mumbled something about needing to check the soil, a flimsy excuse that felt pathetic even to my own ears, desperate to end the conversation before the reality of shared space settled in.

[story 3]

The request arrived via a hastily scribbled note tucked under my door. It detailed her plans for heirloom roses and basil, written in looping, enthusiastic script. Reading it, a wave of nausea washed over me. I reread the words three times, the ink blurring slightly under my scrutiny. When I finally stepped out to confront her, the sun felt too bright, the air too heavy. "I appreciate you thinking of me," I managed, my voice coming out thin and reedy. I avoided looking directly at the area she indicated, focusing instead on the pattern of the paving stones. My shoulders were tense, hiked up near my ears, and I kept shifting my weight from one foot to the other, a restless energy vibrating just beneath my skin, as if I were waiting for something unpleasant to happen.
